title: The Presidential Election and the Nostalgia of '48
summary: Truman, the bland underdog from the Middle West, came out swinging in the election campaign, fustigating the "do-nothing" Republican Congress and promising to "give 'em hell!" We see certain parallels today in what was the abortive—and absurd—attempt on the part of largely Tea Party Republicans in the House to deny the extension of tax cuts to 160 million Americans.
body: My head tells me that Mitt Romney is going to be elected President in 2012. The results of the Iowa caucuses have done nothing to dispel this notion. Romney is moderate, he keeps to his own wife (however contrary to Nature such comportment may be), and he gives the impression of being able to handle the economy. Further, the Mormon factor, like many prejudices that get transformed with time and exposure, appears not as consequential as it seemed in 2008. My heart goes back to the election of 1948 when, like many other Harvard undergraduates, we stayed up all night in our rooms in the Houses (Harvard for dorms), and when dawn came—divine surprise!—Harry Truman was elected President. Truman, the bland underdog from the Middle West, came out swinging in the election campaign, fustigating the "do-nothing" Republican Congress and promising to "give 'em hell!" We see certain parallels today in what was the abortive—and absurd—attempt on the part of largely Tea Party Republicans in the House to deny the extension of tax cuts to 160 million Americans. On the Republican side, though there are certain similarities in persona between Romney and "the man on the wedding cake" of 1948, Romney is less waxy than was his unfortunate co-Republican, Thomas E. Dewey. While much of the hate-filled opposition to Barack Obama represents a repressed racism, the President has not risen to the bait. He appears preternaturally calm and is unresponsive to the barbs that are being constantly thrown at him. He remains the very model of an African-American President. That may not, however, be sufficient to get re-elected in 2012. Statements and views expressed in this op-ed are solely those of the author and do not imply endorsement by Harvard University, Harvard Kennedy School, or the Belfer Center for Science and International Affairs. result.main_content_en: The article reflects on the 2012 U.S. presidential election through the lens of historical nostalgia, specifically the 1948 election. The author's analytical 'head' predicts a Mitt Romney victory, citing his moderate image, perceived economic competence, and the diminishing significance of his Mormon faith as factors. Romney is contrasted with the 1948 Republican candidate, Thomas E. Dewey, being seen as less rigid. The author's emotional 'heart' recalls the surprise victory of Harry Truman in 1948, who campaigned aggressively against a 'do-nothing' Congress. This historical parallel is drawn to contemporary politics, referencing the Tea Party Republicans' attempt to block tax cut extensions. The piece also analyzes President Barack Obama's demeanor, describing him as preternaturally calm in the face of racially-tinged opposition, while questioning if this composure will be enough for re-election. The core argument juxtaposes cold political analysis with the powerful, often unpredictable, force of voter sentiment and historical resonance.
result.main_content_zh: 本文透过对1948年大选的历史怀旧视角，反思2012年美国总统选举。作者理性分析认为米特·罗姆尼将获胜，理由是其温和形象、 perceived economic competence、以及其摩门教信仰因素的重要性已较2008年减弱。罗姆尼被拿来与1948年共和党候选人托马斯·杜威对比，被认为不那么刻板。作者的情感则回溯到1948年哈里·杜鲁门的意外胜选，其当年曾猛烈抨击“无所作为”的国会并积极竞选。这一历史类比被用于当代政治，提及茶党共和党人试图阻止减税政策延期。文章还分析了巴拉克·奥巴马总统的应对方式，描述其在充满种族色彩的反对声中异常冷静，但同时质疑这种镇定是否足以助其连任。核心论点是冷峻的政治分析与选民情绪及历史共鸣的强大且往往不可预测的力量之间的并置。
